text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Role-Based AI Use Guidelines lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Different public health roles need different AI permissions, responsibilities, training expectations, review duties, and escalation pathways.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Different public health roles need different AI permissions, responsibilities, training expectations, review duties, and escalation pathways.
textPreview: Different public health roles need different AI permissions, responsibilities, training expectations, review duties, and escalation pathw...

text: Role clarity prevents inconsistent AI use, unsupported staff experimentation, unreviewed public communications, and unclear accountability.
textPreview: Role clarity prevents inconsistent AI use, unsupported staff experimentation, unreviewed public communications, and unclear accountability.

text: A one-size-fits-all AI policy can leave staff unclear about what they are allowed to do and what they must escalate.
textPreview: A one-size-fits-all AI policy can leave staff unclear about what they are allowed to do and what they must escalate.

text:
Use this slide in the Role-Based AI Use Guidelines training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 5, 6, 10, 12 and Tools 2, 23, 24, 26, 27, 28, 29.
Emphasize the core guardrail: A one-size-fits-all AI policy can leave staff unclear about what they are allowed to do and what they must escalate.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Role-Based AI Use Guidelines in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Role-Based AI Use Guidelines training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 5, 6, 10, 12 and Tools 2, 23, 24, 26, 27, 28, 29.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Role-based AI guidelines translate policy into daily practice. Leaders, program staff, analysts, communications staff, IT/security, privacy/legal, procurement, equity staff, supervisors, and governance members each need practical guidance for their responsibilities.

text:
Use this slide in the Role-Based AI Use Guidelines training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 5, 6, 10, 12 and Tools 2, 23, 24, 26, 27, 28, 29.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
Which roles need permission to use which AI tools? Which roles must review outputs before they are used? Which roles need advanced training before governance or procurement decisions?
